did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a hybrid framework for skin cancer detection using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for feature extraction and multiple machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, SGD, SVM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, K-Nearest Neighbors) to classify dermatoscopic images into benign or malignant categories. It also employed Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically SHAP and t-SNE, for model interpretability and feature analysis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a hybrid CNN framework for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods including data collection from Kaggle (3,297 images), feature extraction, and the application of multiple machine learning algorithms (Logistic Regression, SGD, SVM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, KNN). It reports original results such as cross-validation accuracy metrics (e.g., Gradient Boosting 0.8380 ± 0.0159) and uses XAI techniques (SHAP, t-SNE). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICIC 2025), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hybrid CNN Framework for Enhanced Skin Cancer Detection: Merging Machine Learning and Explainable AI
journal: 2025 Tenth International Conference on Informatics and Computing (ICIC)
brief_summary: This study developed a hybrid framework for skin cancer detection that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for feature extraction with multiple machine learning classifiers, including Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset of 3,297 dermatoscopic images sourced from Kaggle, categorized into benign and malignant classes. The primary evaluation approach involved cross-validation to assess accuracy, precision, and recall, alongside the application of Explainable AI (XAI) techniques such as SHAP and t-SNE for interpretability. The Gradient Boosting model achieved the best performance among the tested algorithms, with a cross-validation accuracy of 0.8380 ± 0.0159 and both precision and recall values of 0.97 for benign and malignant classes. The findings demonstrate that combining CNN-based feature extraction with machine learning classifiers, enhanced by XAI methods, can improve diagnostic reliability and trust in AI-generated predictions for dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification categories in the dataset, comprising 1,800 images.
One of the two binary classification categories in the dataset, comprising 1,497 images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermatoscopic images into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The study classifies skin cancer images into two discrete diagnostic categories: benign (Class 0) and malignant (Class 1). The primary task involves using a hybrid CNN framework with multiple machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, SGD, SVM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, KNN) to predict these binary labels. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images sourced from the Kaggle website, used for training and validating machine learning models for skin cancer detection.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN framework and multiple machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, SGD, SVM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, KNN) via Stratified K-Fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation through Stratified K-Fold cross-validation (5-fold) to evaluate model performance and optimize parameters.
use.testing: Used for testing via the hold-out folds of the Stratified K-Fold cross-validation to assess precision, recall, and accuracy.
use.other: Used for feature extraction performance analysis using t-SNE and Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) to visualize and understand feature distributions between classes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance and ensure generalization by splitting the dataset into five balanced folds, maintaining class proportions and reducing bias through shuffling.
To report the central tendency and variability of cross-validation accuracy scores for each machine learning model (e.g., Logistic Regression 0.8356 ± 0.0077, Gradient Boosting 0.8380 ± 0.0159).
To estimate the probability distribution of extracted features and visualize feature density differences between benign and malignant classes.
To reduce the dimensionality of high-dimensional CNN features for visualization and to assess the separability/clustering of benign and malignant classes in two-dimensional space.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation of cross-validation accuracy) and classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset.metric: Cross-Validation Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score (Class 0 Benign, Class 1 Malignant), Overall Accuracy, Macro Avg F1, Weighted Avg F1
Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: Gradient Boosting was identified as the best performing model among those with practical resource constraints, achieving high precision and recall (0.97) for both classes. XGBoost achieved perfect scores (1.00) but was noted as less reliable due to insufficient system resources. KNN had the lowest performance. Logistic Regression showed high precision for benign cases (0.93) and good recall for malignant cases (0.92). SGD Classifier had high precision for benign cases (0.97) and high recall for malignant cases (0.97). SVM (RBF) performed below average in distinguishing classes. The study utilized Stratified K-Fold cross-validation (5-fold) for these results.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid framework combining a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for feature extraction with multiple classical machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, SGD Classifier, SVM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, K-Nearest Neighbors).

classifiers.name: Gradient Boosting
classifiers.description: Leverages algorithmic error differences from previous models

classifiers.name: XGBoost
classifiers.description: Efficient gradient boosting, assessed using log loss without label encoding

classification_or_output_head.details: The CNN outputs a numpy array of 19,200 features which are fed into the respective ML classifiers for binary classification (Benign vs Malignant).

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 160x120 pixels using transforms.Resize()
Conversion to torch tensor using transforms.ToTensor()
Normalization using transforms.Normalize() with mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and standard deviation [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]
conclusions: The Gradient Boosting model achieved the best performance among the tested classifiers, with a cross-validation accuracy of 0.8380 ± 0.0159 and both precision and recall values of 0.97 for benign and malignant classes.

The hybrid framework combining CNN-based feature extraction with machine learning classifiers effectively distinguishes between benign and malignant skin lesions, as evidenced by meaningful latent representations captured by the CNN.

The use of Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically SHAP and t-SNE, enhances the interpretability of the model, providing healthcare professionals with insights into feature contributions and improving trust in AI-generated predictions.

While XGBoost achieved perfect classification scores (1.00) on the dataset, it was noted as less reliable due to insufficient system resources during evaluation, whereas Gradient Boosting offered a more practical balance of performance and resource constraints.
The study demonstrates that merging deep learning (CNN) for feature extraction with traditional machine learning classifiers can improve diagnostic accuracy and reliability in skin cancer detection.
The findings suggest that the proposed hybrid approach can help inform and improve clinical decisions by providing interpretable and accurate diagnostic tools for dermatology.

The model showed the ability to generalize to unseen data through Stratified K-Fold cross-validation, although the authors note that additional optimizations and better data could further improve generalization.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, small public dataset (3,297 images from Kaggle) with no external validation, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

XGBoost achieved perfect classification scores (1.00 accuracy, precision, recall, F1) but was explicitly noted by the authors as 'less reliable due to insufficient system resources,' suggesting potential data leakage or overfitting artifacts rather than true performance.

The best-performing model, Gradient Boosting, achieved a cross-validation accuracy of only 0.8380 ± 0.0159, which is modest for skin cancer detection tasks compared to state-of-the-art deep learning models.
The CNN architecture used for feature extraction is custom and shallow (3 convolutional layers), which may limit the depth of feature representation compared to pre-trained backbone networks.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to the limited performance and risk of overfitting given the small dataset size.
The dataset is imbalanced (1,800 benign vs. 1,497 malignant), although Stratified K-Fold cross-validation was used to mitigate class imbalance during evaluation.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values) to compare model performances, relying solely on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation).
Clinical relevance is limited as the study does not involve dermatologist validation or comparison against human expert performance.
Deployment concerns are not addressed; the study focuses on offline model evaluation rather than real-time inference constraints or integration into clinical workflows.
